payload.formula_text:
Days Until Exhaustion = Budget Remaining / Daily Run Rate
Daily Run Rate = AVG(Daily Spend) over last 14 days
payload.formula_explanation: Predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on your current spending velocity. Uses your 14-day average daily spend as the run rate. Compares exhaustion date against days remaining in the month to identify risk.
how_to_read: The large number shows days until budget runs out at current rate
Green means you'll have budget through month end
Yellow means exhaustion 10-20% earlier than month end
Red means you'll run out significantly before month end
Budget Remaining shows dollars left to spend
Daily Run Rate shows your current spending velocity
Exhaustion Date shows the projected day budget hits zero
decisions_answered: Will our budget last through the end of the month?
How many days of budget do we have at current rate?
Do we need to reduce daily spend to stretch the budget?
What's our spending runway if we don't make changes?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI card predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on current spending velocity.

The formula: Budget remaining divided by your 14-day average daily spend gives days until exhaustion.

Green means you're on track - budget will last through month end. Yellow is a warning - you'll run out 10 to 20 percent earlier than expected. Red is critical - take action or budget runs out prematurely.

The exhaustion date shows the specific day budget hits zero. Compare this to days remaining in the month.

This answers: Will budget last the month? How many days of runway do we have? Do we need to slow spending to stretch the budget?

payload.formula_text:
ROAS 14-Day = Revenue (14d) / Spend (14d)
Recommendation = INCREASE if Platform ROAS > Avg ROAS x 1.2
Recommendation = DECREASE if Platform ROAS < Avg ROAS x 0.8
payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes 14-day platform performance and recommends budget adjustments. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, it recommends increasing budget by 20%. If ROAS is 20% below average, it recommends decreasing by 15%. Helps optimize budget allocation based on efficiency.
how_to_read: Each card shows a platform with its recommendation
Green INCREASE means allocate more budget to this platform
Red DECREASE means reduce budget from this platform
Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is appropriate
14-Day ROAS shows recent efficiency for the platform
Current vs Recommended shows suggested budget change
Rationale explains the reasoning behind recommendation
decisions_answered: Should we shift budget between Google and Meta?
Which platform deserves more investment?
Is our current budget allocation optimal?
What's the expected benefit of reallocation?
payload.narration_text:
These recommendation cards suggest budget reallocation based on 14-day platform performance.

The formula: Calculate each platform's ROAS over 14 days. Compare to average. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, recommend increasing its budget. If 20% below, recommend decreasing.

Green INCREASE means this platform is efficient - give it more budget. Red DECREASE means it's underperforming - shift budget away. Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is fine.

The rationale explains why: high ROAS suggests room to scale profitably. Low ROAS suggests diminishing returns.

This answers: Should we rebalance between platforms? Which deserves more investment? Is our allocation optimal?

payload.formula_text:
Daily Spend = Google Spend + Meta Spend
7-Day Moving Average = AVG(Daily Spend) OVER 7 DAYS
payload.formula_explanation: Shows 30 days of daily spend as a stacked area chart. Google Ads spend is shown in blue, Meta Ads in lighter blue stacked on top. The moving average line smooths out daily fluctuations to reveal spending trends. The budget reference line shows daily target.
how_to_read: Blue area represents Google Ads daily spend
Light blue stacked area represents Meta Ads daily spend
Total height shows combined daily spend
The dashed horizontal line marks your daily budget target
Weekend periods may show lower spend (depending on campaigns)
The moving average line reveals spending trends
Hover for exact daily values by platform
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily spending pattern?
Are there spending anomalies or sudden changes?
Which platform contributes more to daily spend?
Do weekends show different spending patterns?
Is spend trending up or down over the month?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows daily spend over the last 30 days, broken down by platform.

The blue area is Google Ads spend. Stacked on top in lighter blue is Meta Ads spend. Total height shows combined daily spend.

The dashed line marks your daily budget target. When the area exceeds this line, you overspent that day. When it falls short, you underspent.

Look for patterns: consistent spending helps campaign algorithms learn. Sudden drops might indicate budget exhaustion or paused campaigns. Weekend dips are normal for some businesses.

This answers: How consistent is spending? Are there anomalies? Which platform dominates daily spend?

payload.formula_text:
Actual Spend = Google Spend + Meta Spend
MoM Variance % = ((Current Month - Previous Month) / Previous Month) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows historical monthly spend over the last 6 months. Reveals spending patterns, seasonality, and trends. Month-over-month variance helps understand typical spending fluctuations and forecast accuracy.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one month's total spend
Taller bars indicate higher spending months
Look for patterns: Q1 typically higher for some businesses
MoM variance shows spending growth or decline trend
Hover for exact dollar amounts
Use historical patterns to improve future forecasts
decisions_answered: What has our monthly spend been historically?
Are there seasonal patterns in our spending?
Is spend trending up or down over time?
How much variance should we expect month-to-month?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows monthly spend history over the last 6 months.

The formula: total monthly spend is Google plus Meta combined. Month-over-month variance shows percentage change from previous month.

Look for patterns: many e-commerce businesses spend heavily in Q1 during sales events, then normalize in Q2. Understanding your patterns helps set realistic budgets.

Large month-over-month swings indicate either intentional budget changes or inconsistent spend management. Steady growth suggests scaling strategy.

This answers: What's our spend history? Are there seasonal patterns? Is spend trending up or down? How much variance is typical?

payload.formula_text:
Actual Pacing % = (MTD Spend / Monthly Budget) x 100
Expected % = (Day of Month / Days in Month) x 100
Projected Spend = MTD Spend + (Avg Daily x Days Remaining)
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks month-to-date spend and projects end-of-month total based on current run rate. The projection uses your average daily spend to estimate final monthly spend. Status indicates whether you'll hit, exceed, or fall short of budget.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual MTD spend percentage
The marker shows expected percentage based on day of month
Projected Spend shows estimated month-end total at current rate
Days Remaining shows business days left in the month
Status badge shows ON_TRACK, OVER_PACING, or UNDER_PACING
Color indicates projected variance from monthly budget
decisions_answered: Will we hit our monthly budget target at current spending rate?
How much budget do we have remaining this month?
What daily spend rate do we need to hit the monthly target?
Are we trending to over-spend or under-spend for the month?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar shows month-to-date budget pacing with end-of-month projection.

The formulas: Actual pacing is MTD spend divided by monthly budget. Expected pacing accounts for which day of the month we're on. Projection multiplies average daily spend by days remaining.

The status badge tells you the key insight: ON TRACK means you'll hit budget within 5%. OVER PACING means you'll exceed budget. UNDER PACING means you'll fall short.

This answers: Will we hit monthly budget? How much remains? What daily rate do we need to finish on target?

payload.formula_text:
Spend Ratio = Daily Spend / Daily Budget
CRITICAL: Ratio > 1.15 or < 0.70
WARNING: Ratio > 1.05 or < 0.85
payload.formula_explanation: Monitors daily spend against budget and generates alerts when significant deviations occur. Critical alerts indicate >15% overspend or >30% underspend. Warning alerts catch moderate deviations of 5-15%. Helps proactive budget management.
how_to_read: Red CRITICAL alerts require immediate attention
Yellow WARNING alerts need monitoring
Blue INFO alerts are notable but not urgent
Each alert shows the date and specific issue
Dollar amounts show actual spend vs budget for that day
Empty state (green checkmark) means budget is on track
Alerts sorted by date, most recent first
decisions_answered: Were there any days with significant overspend?
Are there patterns in budget deviations?
Which days need investigation for spend issues?
Is overall budget management healthy or problematic?
payload.narration_text:
This alert stack shows budget deviations requiring attention over the last 7 days.

The formula: daily spend divided by daily budget gives the spend ratio. Critical alerts trigger when ratio exceeds 1.15 or falls below 0.70. Warning alerts trigger at 1.05 or 0.85.

Red critical alerts need immediate attention - you're significantly over or under budget. Yellow warnings need monitoring but aren't urgent. Blue info alerts are just notable observations.

If you see a green checkmark and no alerts, congratulations - your budget management is healthy.

This answers: Were there overspend days? Are there deviation patterns? Which days need investigation?

payload.formula_text:
Platform Pacing % = (Platform MTD Spend / Platform Monthly Budget) x 100
Platform ROAS = Platform Revenue / Platform Spend
payload.formula_explanation: Compares pacing between Google Ads and Meta Ads. Shows whether platforms are spending at the same rate or if one is significantly ahead or behind. Includes ROAS comparison to understand efficiency differences.
how_to_read: Progress bars show pacing percentage for each platform
Blue bar represents Google Ads pacing
Light blue bar represents Meta Ads pacing
The marker shows expected pacing for the day of month
Grid shows MTD Spend, Budget, Daily Rate, and ROAS
ROAS badges are color-coded: green 8x+, yellow 6-8x, red <6x
Status badges show ON_TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each platform
decisions_answered: Which platform is pacing faster - Google or Meta?
Should we rebalance budget between platforms?
Which platform has better ROAS efficiency?
Is one platform significantly over or under-spending?
payload.narration_text:
This comparison shows budget pacing for Google Ads versus Meta Ads side by side.

The formula: each platform's MTD spend divided by its monthly budget gives the pacing percentage. ROAS is revenue divided by spend for each platform.

Progress bars show how each platform is tracking. The marker shows where they should be based on the day of month. Status badges indicate ON TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each.

Compare the ROAS values carefully. If Meta has 13x ROAS but Google has 8x, Meta is more efficient per dollar spent.

This answers: Which platform paces faster? Should you rebalance? Which has better efficiency? Is one significantly off track?

payload.formula_text:
Projected Revenue = MTD Revenue + (Avg Daily Revenue x Days Left)
Current ROAS = MTD Revenue / MTD Spend
Projected ROAS = Projected Revenue / Projected Spend
payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month revenue based on current revenue trajectory. ROAS indicators show whether efficiency is maintaining, improving, or declining. This helps forecast not just spend, but the return on that spend.
how_to_read: The bullet chart shows projected revenue relative to targets
Current ROAS shows efficiency of spend to date
Projected ROAS shows expected month-end efficiency
Green ROAS (8x+) indicates excellent efficiency
Yellow ROAS (6-8x) indicates acceptable efficiency
Red ROAS (<6x) indicates efficiency concerns
Compare Current vs Projected ROAS for trend direction
decisions_answered: What will our total revenue be at month end?
Is our ROAS holding steady or changing?
Are we getting good return on our ad spend?
Should we adjust budget based on revenue efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
This bullet chart projects end-of-month revenue with ROAS efficiency indicators.

The formula: MTD revenue plus average daily revenue times days remaining. ROAS is calculated as revenue divided by spend for both current and projected values.

ROAS color coding: green means 8x or better - excellent efficiency. Yellow means 6 to 8x - acceptable. Red means below 6x - efficiency concerns.

Compare current ROAS to projected ROAS. If projected is lower, efficiency is declining. If higher, you're improving.

This answers: What will month-end revenue be? Is ROAS holding steady? Should we adjust budget based on efficiency trends?

payload.formula_text:
Simple Projection = MTD Spend + (Avg Daily x Days Left)
Weighted Projection = MTD + (Weekday Avg x Weekdays Left) + (Weekend Avg x Weekends Left)
Confidence Range = Projection +/- (Std Dev x Days Left)
payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month spend using multiple methods. Simple projection uses overall daily average. Weighted projection accounts for weekday versus weekend spending patterns. Confidence range shows expected variance based on historical spending volatility.
how_to_read: The bullet chart shows projected spend relative to budget target
Green zone means projection is within 5% of budget
Yellow zone means 5-10% variance from budget
Red zone means >10% variance - attention needed
The target marker shows your monthly budget
Range values show low-high confidence interval
Current shows MTD actual, Projected shows month-end estimate
decisions_answered: What will our total spend be at month end?
How confident are we in the projection?
Are we going to be over or under budget?
What's the best and worst case spending scenario?
payload.narration_text:
This bullet chart projects your end-of-month spend with confidence intervals.

Multiple formulas provide the projection: simple average uses overall daily spend. Weighted projection accounts for weekday versus weekend differences. The confidence range uses standard deviation to show expected variance.

The bar shows projected spend. The marker shows your monthly budget target. Color zones indicate how close you are to target: green within 5%, yellow 5 to 10% off, red more than 10% variance.

The range shows low and high scenarios based on spending volatility.

This answers: What will month-end spend be? How confident is the projection? Will we be over or under budget?

payload.formula_text:
Pacing % = (Today's Spend / Daily Budget) x 100
Expected Pacing % = (Current Hour / 24) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: The gauge shows how much of your daily budget has been spent relative to how much time has passed today. If you've spent 50% of budget by noon (50% of the day), you're on track. Over 115% or under 85% of expected pacing triggers warning indicators.
how_to_read: The needle shows your current pacing percentage (0-150%)
The marker line indicates expected pacing based on time of day
Green zone (85-115%) means you're on track
Orange zones (70-85% or 115-130%) indicate warning
Red zones (<70% or >130%) indicate critical deviation
Bottom values show actual spend vs daily budget
decisions_answered: Are we spending too fast or too slow today?
Will we exhaust our daily budget before day end?
Should we adjust campaign bids mid-day?
Is today's spend aligned with time of day expectations?
payload.narration_text:
This gauge shows today's budget pacing in real-time, updated every 15 minutes.

The formula divides today's spend by your total daily budget, times 100. A second formula calculates expected pacing based on time of day.

To read this: the needle shows current pacing percentage. The marker line shows where you should be based on the hour. Green means on track. Orange means moderate deviation. Red means critical - take action.

This answers: Are we spending too fast or slow? Will we exhaust budget early? Should we adjust bids mid-day to stay on track?

payload.formula_text:
Weekly Pacing % = (WTD Spend / Weekly Budget) x 100
Expected % = (Days Elapsed / 7) x 100
WoW Change = ((Projected - Last Week) / Last Week) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows week-to-date spend progress against the weekly budget target. The expected pacing line accounts for which day of the week we're on. Week-over-week comparison shows spending trajectory changes.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual spend percentage (solid fill)
The vertical marker shows expected pacing for current day of week
Green bar if within 10% of expected, yellow if 10-20% off, red if >20%
WTD Spend shows total spent so far this week
Days Remaining shows workdays left to hit weekly target
WoW Change shows projected spend versus last week
decisions_answered: How is this week's spend tracking against last week?
Do we need to adjust daily spending to hit weekly targets?
Are there any days where we significantly over/under-spent?
What's our spending velocity trend week-over-week?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar tracks week-to-date spending against your weekly budget.

The formula: week-to-date spend divided by weekly budget, times 100. The expected marker shows where you should be based on the day of week.

Color coding helps: green means within 10% of expected. Yellow means 10 to 20% variance. Red means more than 20% off track.

Week-over-week change shows if you're trending higher or lower than last week. This helps answer: Are we on pace this week? Do we need to adjust daily spend? How does this compare to last week?
